event: Mesolithic hunter-gatherers begin seasonally inhabiting the high Pyrenean valleys that would become Andorra.
The Balma de la Margineda rock shelter near Sant Julia de Loria is occupied, leaving one of the region's most important prehistoric archaeological records.
Bronze Age communities establish more permanent settlements and practice transhumant pastoralism in the valleys.
The Iberian tribe of the Andosins inhabits the valleys, a people later cited by ancient authors as the likely origin of Andorra's name.
Hannibal's army crosses the Pyrenees during the Second Punic War, with the Andosins reputedly resisting the Carthaginian advance.
Roman influence reaches the valleys as the Pyrenees are incorporated into the orbit of the Roman Empire.
With the decline of Rome, the valleys come under the sway of the Visigoths in the Iberian Peninsula.
Muslim forces of the Umayyad conquest reach the southern Pyrenees, drawing the region into the frontier between Islam and Christendom.
Tradition holds that Charlemagne grants the people of the valleys a charter for their help against the Moors, giving rise to Andorra's founding legend.
Louis the Pious, Charlemagne's son, is said to have confirmed the privileges of the Andorran valleys in a charter.
The Act of Consecration of the Cathedral of Urgell mentions the Andorran parishes, providing the earliest documentary reference to the valleys.
Count Borrell II of Urgell transfers the Andorran valleys to the Diocese of Urgell, placing them under the authority of the bishops.
Count Ermengol VI of Urgell cedes his remaining rights over Andorra to the Bishop of Urgell, consolidating ecclesiastical lordship.
The bishops entrust the protection of Andorra to the noble Caboet family, beginning the lineage that would lead to shared overlordship.
Through marriage, the rights of the Caboet family pass to the Counts of Foix, creating rival claims with the Bishop of Urgell.
The first paréage is signed between the Bishop of Urgell and the Count of Foix, establishing Andorra's enduring co-principality of two co-princes.
A second paréage refines the arrangement, settling disputes over rights such as military levies and judicial authority in the valleys.
The co-princes authorize the creation of the Consell de la Terra, an assembly of valley representatives that becomes one of Europe's oldest parliaments.
An edict of French King Henry IV establishes the head of the French state as co-prince, after Foix's rights had passed to the French crown.
Antoni Fiter i Rossell compiles the Manual Digest, codifying Andorran customs, laws, and traditions in a foundational reference work.
The Politar Andorra is written, further documenting the institutions and governance of the valleys.
Revolutionary France renounces the French co-princeship and feudal dues, briefly suspending the historic arrangement during the French Revolution.
Emperor Napoleon I restores the French co-princeship at the request of the Andorrans, preserving the co-principality's dual structure.
The Nova Reforma broadens suffrage among male heads of household and modernizes Andorra's institutions under Guillem d'Areny-Plandolit.
Electricity and other modern infrastructure begin slowly reaching the isolated principality in the late 19th century.
Demands for democratic reform spark unrest; universal male suffrage is granted and French gendarmes briefly intervene to restore order.
Russian adventurer Boris Skossyreff proclaims himself King Boris I of Andorra before being arrested and expelled within days.
French forces are stationed in Andorra during the Spanish Civil War to maintain order along the border.
Andorra remains neutral during World War II and becomes a smuggling route for refugees fleeing across the Pyrenees.
Andorra formally declares an end to its state of war with Germany, having been technically belligerent since World War I yet never participating.
Women are granted the right to vote in Andorra, expanding the franchise significantly.
Voting rights are further extended and third-generation residents gain political rights in ongoing reforms.
Andorra's first political party, the Partit Democratic Andorra, is founded as modern political life takes shape.
An executive Govern (Council of Ministers) led by a head of government is created, separating executive power from the legislature.
The first Cap de Govern (head of government), Oscar Ribas Reig, takes office under the new executive arrangement.
Andorrans approve their first written constitution in a referendum, establishing a parliamentary democracy while retaining the two co-princes as heads of state.
Andorra joins the United Nations, gaining full international recognition as a sovereign state.
Andorra becomes a member of the Council of Europe, deepening its integration into European institutions.
Andorra joins the World Trade Organization's predecessor frameworks and broadens economic ties amid its tourism-driven economy.
Andorra adopts the euro as its de facto currency, replacing the French franc and Spanish peseta following the eurozone's launch.
Under international pressure, Andorra commits to ending its status as a tax haven and begins reforming its banking secrecy laws.
Andorra signs a monetary agreement with the European Union, formally authorizing its use of the euro and the minting of its own euro coins.
Andorra issues its first national euro coins, marking a milestone in its monetary integration with Europe.
The collapse of Banca Privada d'Andorra (BPA) over money-laundering allegations triggers a major banking sector reform.
Andorra introduces a personal income tax for the first time, completing its shift away from its tax-haven reputation.
Andorra signs an association agreement negotiation framework with the European Union to deepen access to the single market.
Andorra becomes a full member of the International Monetary Fund, joining the global financial system.
Andorra concludes negotiations on an association agreement with the European Union, paving the way for closer integration while preserving its sovereignty.
